ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Vietnamese fish cake hot pot; 'hot pot food product' (FOODON_03315595) used as the closest broader class. Fish cake component maps to FOODON_03302050 (fish cake).
ontology.foodex2_description: Fish and fish products — fish-based dishes
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (fish-based dishes) used as the closest defensible broader category; no specific code exists for Vietnamese fish cake hot pot in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for fish cake hot pot as a prepared dish. SNOMED CT coverage for composite Asian hot pot dishes is absent.

main_ingredients: fish cake (chả cá thác lác / featherback fish cake)

serving.natural_language_portion: One individual serving of fish cake hot pot is approximately 400 g, comprising broth, fish cakes, vegetables, and a portion of rice vermicelli; a shared hot pot set for 2–3 people weighs approximately 1,200 g.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Fish cake, Asian style (SR Legacy) and Fish broth composite; supplemented by Singapore HPB Nutrient Composition of Food for fish cake and hot pot dish analogues
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board, Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Vietnamese fish cake hot pot as a composite dish. Nutrient values were estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central data for fish cake (Asian style, SR Legacy NDB 15209), fish broth, rice vermicelli, and common hot pot vegetables, weighted by typical recipe proportions (~35% fish cake, ~25% broth, ~20% rice vermicelli, ~20% vegetables). The input record's per-100g values (energy 125 kcal, protein 8.33 g, fat 5.0 g, carbohydrate 11.67 g) are consistent with this composite estimate and have been retained with medium confidence. Sodium 375 mg/100g is plausible for a fish-sauce-seasoned broth dish and is consistent with HPB data for similar hot pot preparations.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — rice vermicelli and mixed fish-vegetable hot pot analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Vietnamese fish cake hot pot. GI estimated at 53 based on the dominant carbohydrate source being rice vermicelli (GI ~53 per University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2021, rice noodles entry). Per the standard GI classification scale (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70), a GI of 53 falls in the low category. The fish cake and vegetable components have negligible independent GI contribution. Glycemic load per 400 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (53 × 46.68 g) / 100 ≈ 24.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 375 mg per 100 g; a 400 g serving delivers approximately 1,500 mg sodium, representing 65–75% of the recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce and low-sodium broth, limit broth consumption, and restrict portion size to no more than 200–250 g to keep single-meal sodium below 750 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 11.67 g per 100 g (predominantly from rice vermicelli, GI ~53, low category); protein 8.33 g and dietary fibre 1.25 g per 100 g help moderate postprandial glycaemic response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should choose rice vermicelli over instant noodles, limit portion size to 300–350 g, and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to reduce glycaemic load and improve satiety.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is low at 1.25 g per 100 g and trans fat is negligible at 0.04 g per 100 g; however, sodium at 375 mg per 100 g poses a cardiovascular risk through blood pressure elevation.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion size to limit sodium intake, choose lean fish cake preparations without added lard, and increase the proportion of vegetables relative to noodles to improve the overall cardiovascular risk profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Boiled/simmered preparation minimises advanced glycation end-product (AGE) formation; anti-inflammatory herbs (basil, culantro) and vegetables support RA management.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to nightshades should reduce or omit tomatoes and chilli; all patients should request reduced fish sauce to limit sodium-driven tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fish Cake Hot Pot (Vietnamese: Lẩu chả cá thác lác) is a traditional Vietnamese hot pot dish centred on handmade featherback fish cakes (chả cá thác lác) simmered in a fragrant, savoury fish broth seasoned with lemongrass, fish sauce, and chilli. The dish is served communally at the table over a portable burner, with diners adding raw vegetables — typically water spinach (rau muống), bean sprouts, and tomato — and rice vermicelli directly into the simmering broth. The fish cakes have a firm, springy texture with a mild, slightly sweet fish flavour; the broth is light yet deeply savoury with herbal and citrus notes from lemongrass and culantro. Nutritionally, a 100 g portion provides approximately 125 kcal, 8.3 g protein, 5.0 g fat (1.25 g saturated), and 11.7 g carbohydrate, with sodium at 375 mg — the primary clinical concern. The dish is a low-GI food (GI ~53) due to rice vermicelli as the dominant carbohydrate source. It is suitable for most healthy adults and individuals managing type 2 diabetes in controlled portions, but requires sodium reduction for hypertensive and cardiovascular patients. The boiled/simmered cooking method avoids AGE formation, making it preferable to fried fish cake preparations. Commonly sold in Vietnamese restaurants and food delivery platforms in Singapore and across Southeast Asia.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Vietnamese fish cake hot pot as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for fish cake, fish broth, rice vermicelli, and vegetables, weighted by typical recipe proportions. The input record's values are consistent with this composite estimate. GI is estimated from the rice vermicelli component using the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite estimation approach.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient breakdown, and moisture data for this composite Vietnamese fish cake hot pot dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or the Singapore HPB Nutrient Composition database as a single composite entry as of 2024. Values would require laboratory analysis of a standardised recipe. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was not used as a primary nutrient source as it is a delivery/menu platform; USDA FoodData Central and HPB were used instead. The input record listed 'country_of_origin' as Singapore, which likely reflects the GrabFood listing location; the dish's culinary origin is Vietnam and this has been corrected in region_of_origin. Ethnic tags 'Pho' and 'Banh Mi' from the input have been removed as they are unrelated to this dish. GI classification: a GI of 53 falls in the low category (≤55) per the standard University of Sydney GI classification scale; gi_category corrected from 'medium' to 'low' in iteration 3.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Replaced forbidden GrabFood/foodo.sg nutrient source with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite estimate supplemented by Singapore HPB.
Iteration 1: Added Vietnamese local name 'Lẩu chả cá thác lác' to satisfy G6 Vietnamese cuisine multilingual requirement.
Iteration 1: Added Chinese simplified and traditional names for broader alias coverage.
Iteration 1: Replaced input 'diabetes' condition key with 'type2_diabetes' and human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' per scoring model requirement.
Iteration 1: Added 'cardiovascular_disease' health context condition (was absent in input).
Iteration 1: Documented all -1 fields in notes per G3 negative sentinel values rule.
Iteration 1: Added GI estimate (53) from University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate (rice vermicelli) with full source documentation.
Iteration 1: Corrected region_of_origin from Singapore to Vietnam.
Iteration 1: Removed unrelated ethnic tags (Pho, Banh Mi) from aliases.
Iteration 1: Verified aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases array (q8 self-check).
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology.foodon_match_type and ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'broader' to 'closest_parent'.

Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' — GI value of 53 falls within the low GI range (≤55) per the University of Sydney GI classification scale. Updated gi_source notes and llm_training description to reflect 'low-GI' classification. Updated notes field to document the correction.
